label: SES-051 work_ticket entity schema designed (audit-and-adopt): 1 session, 6 decisions (DEC-141..146), 0 planning items, 7 references

session.title: Work_ticket entity schema designed; fourth per-entity governance schema specification produced (audit-and-adopt)
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai audit-and-adopt conversation for the work_ticket entity schema — fourth per-entity schema-design conversation in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Opened against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-work-ticket.md. The kickoff specified the standard schema-design cadence (one architectural decision at a time, drive toward governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md). Doug's first instruction in the conversation directed an explicit mode override: 'confirm. then process entire prompt using your default decisions, and don't ask for feedback until all is completed.' The mode shifted from interactive ARCHITECTURE to autonomous-completion against the kickoff's task statement. On the first pre-flight inspection, the deliverable file (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md, 471 lines, dated 05-21-26 18:00 in its header) was present in the local sandbox clone as an untracked file — authored by a prior Claude.ai sandbox session whose work product was never committed to origin/main. This differs from SES-050's audit-and-finalize posture (where the prior file was already at origin) — here the file existed only in sandbox state. The conversation transitioned to audit-and-adopt mode: structured audit pass of the existing untracked draft against the schema spec guide §7.1 completeness gate, §6 cross-spec consistency requirements, the workstream master plan's position-4-of-6 constraints, DEC-117's family-2 classification, the predecessor specs (workstream.md, conversation.md, reference_book.md) declarations the spec relies on, and the kickoff's explicit architectural-question inventory. The audit concluded the draft is substantively complete, methodologically disciplined, internally consistent, and passes every completeness and consistency criterion without correction. No content changes were required. The draft was adopted as v1.0, committed to origin/main, and the close-out payload and apply prompt authored. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `work_ticket` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md — the complete schema specification per the template in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern."

Topics worked through:

1. Pre-flight orientation. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo state at session open. Confirmed `crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md` is the session-startup entry per Doug's confirmation. `git pull --rebase origin main` reported the local clone up to date; HEAD at `31e7afa` (PI-023 apply commit). On the first `git ls-files` and `git status` check of `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/`, found that the three predecessor specs (workstream.md, conversation.md, reference_book.md) are tracked at origin, while `work_ticket.md` (471 lines, dated 05-21-26 18:00, 88KB) was present in the sandbox working directory as an untracked file — authored by a prior Claude.ai sandbox session whose work product was never committed. Cross-checked the V2 db-export: decisions through DEC-140, sessions through SES-050, planning items through PI-023. No DEC-141..146 or SES-051 yet. Surfaced the audit-and-adopt context.

2. Mode override and posture. Doug's first instruction was 'confirm. then process entire prompt using your default decisions, and don't ask for feedback until all is completed.' This switched the conversation from interactive ARCHITECTURE-mode one-decision-at-a-time cadence to autonomous-completion. The audit-and-adopt posture for the pre-existing untracked draft follows naturally from this mode plus the substantive completeness of the draft on inspection.

3. Audit execution. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, governance-schema-workstream-plan.md, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, the three predecessor schema specs in full, DEC-117 from db-export/decisions.json, sessions SES-048/049/050 from db-export/sessions.json (predecessor schema-design conversations), the ses_050.json close-out payload as template, and the work_ticket.md draft itself in full. Audited against four dimensions: (a) schema-spec methodology guide §7.1 per-spec completeness gate — all nine §3 subsections present and substantively filled (§3.1 identity, §3.2 fields with six subsections, §3.3 relationships with four subsections, §3.4 lifecycle with seven subsections, §3.5 API surface with two subsections, §3.6 user-interface with six subsections, §3.7 acceptance criteria with sixteen testable statements, §3.8 open questions with three category sub-buckets, §3.9 cross-references with five subsections), structure matches reference_book.md and conversation.md 1:1 with the appropriate workflow-vs-documentary deviations applied; (b) §6 cross-spec consistency requirements — identifier prefix style (`WT`, two-letter, parallel to WS/PI), format (`WT-NNN`), status field naming (`work_ticket_status`), enum values with `drafted` starter status, source-first relationship-kind naming (DEC-048), parent-prefix field naming (DEC-046), per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-126 (workflow-shaped lifecycle), supersession-requires-edge per DEC-125, soft-delete posture per V2 base behavior, server-side identifier auto-assignment per DEC-128, and V2 `{data, meta, errors}` envelope all conformant; (c) workstream master plan position-4-of-6 constraints — the spec inherits five cross-spec precedents named in §1, establishes one new precedent (terminal-state consumption requires the inbound consumption edge — the inverse of the supersession-requires-edge pattern), and contributes zero new relationship-kind vocabulary (only the entity type itself, already named by conversation.md); (d) predecessor-spec consistency — conversation.md's pre-declared `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` outbound kind (line 180) is correctly inherited as work_ticket's inbound consumption edge, the tentative kind name `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` (named in conversation.md §3.3.2 as candidate) is declined as redundant per DEC-132's tentative-kind-name resolution precedent, and the declined typed read-list kind `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` is justified by DEC-133's frequency-justified test.

4. Audit findings. The draft passes the per-spec completeness gate on every criterion and conforms to the §6 cross-spec consistency requirements without deviation. The workflow-shaped lifecycle (five statuses with three terminals) is the appropriate counterpart to reference_book's documentary-shaped lifecycle, with the documentary-vs-workflow distinction settled by DEC-137 applied on work_ticket's own facts: work_ticket is workflow-shaped (operational transitions are real-time-meaningful events asked about by consultants), so per-status timestamps per DEC-126 apply. The new cross-spec precedent — consumed-requires-edge — is well-motivated as the inbound-direction analogue of the outbound supersession-requires-edge rule from DEC-125 (and structurally parallel to conversation.md's outbound complete-requires-session-edge from DEC-131); the generalization to 'terminal-state-edge requirement, direction determined by the relationship's canonical verb orientation' is the principled framing the remaining schemas (close_out_payload's anticipated `applied` terminal) inherit. The single-use enforcement rule (at most one inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge, regardless of status) is the schema-level realization of DEC-117's family-2 definition and is what distinguishes work_ticket from reference_book at the schema level. The boundary-with-reference_book discipline (intent-at-creation classification, no in-place re-categorization, file_path may coexist across both tables) cleanly resolves the kickoff's explicit three-way boundary question. Sixteen acceptance criteria captured (gate requires ten); each is concrete and testable. No substantive correction required; the draft is adopted as v1.0 without changes.

5. Close-out payload and apply prompt authored. This payload (ses_051.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-051.md) drafted in one pass. Identifier check: latest applied SES is SES-050, so SES-051 is next-available; latest DEC is DEC-140, so DEC-141..146 is next-available for the six decisions; no new planning items in this payload (PI-022 covers retroactive backfill including this conversation's records; PI-023 covers the workstream-state reconciliation utility, applied at commit 31e7afa).

6. Decisions authored (DEC-141..146 — see the decisions array below):
   - DEC-141: work_ticket identifier prefix and format — WT-NNN, two-letter form per DEC-123 precedent, no collision with WS (differs in second character with no visual-shape ambiguity) or any other existing/pending prefix, working-assumption confirmed over WKT/TIX/TKT alternatives.
   - DEC-142: Boundary discipline with reference_book — intent-at-creation classification, no in-place re-categorization, same file_path may coexist as work_ticket_file_path and reference_book_file_path across the two tables (each provides the governance view appropriate to its lifecycle). The methodology workstream master-plan boundary case raised in the kickoff (`ui-v0.4-planning-prompt.md`, kickoff for SES-011 now retroactively cited by successor planning docs) resolves as a work_ticket; the retroactive citations are archival mention, not authoritative reads.
   - DEC-143: work_ticket workflow-shaped lifecycle with five statuses (drafted, ready, consumed, cancelled, superseded), truly-terminal terminals inherited from DEC-125, supersession-requires-edge rule inherited from DEC-125, and a new consumed-requires-edge access-layer rule mirroring the inverse pattern. Establishes the new cross-spec precedent: terminal-state consumption requires the inbound consumption edge (the inverse direction of supersession-requires-edge); generalizes to 'any governance entity with a terminal state defined by an external act of consumption or application requires the inbound edge naming the consumer or applier to be present.' Close_out_payload's anticipated `applied` terminal is the next case.
   - DEC-144: work_ticket single-use enforcement rule — at most one inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge per work_ticket, regardless of status. The rule realizes DEC-117's family-2 definition (single-use seed documents have at most one consumer) as a schema-level invariant. Distinguishes work_ticket from reference_book at the schema level (reference_book admits arbitrarily many inbound edges; work_ticket admits exactly one of the consumption kind).
   - DEC-145: work_ticket field inventory with eleven columns (identity, content, classification, no FK fields, file pointer, base timestamps plus four per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped lifecycle per DEC-126). Declines the tentative inbound kind `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` named by conversation.md §3.3.2 as redundant — the existing outbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` declared in conversation.md is the sole canonical edge, viewed from work_ticket's side as inbound. Declines a typed `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` kind for kickoff-prompt read-list citations on DEC-133's frequency-justified test — read-lists are operational metadata rather than queried-by structure; the generic `references` kind suffices.
   - DEC-146: work_ticket API surface (standard 8-endpoint set with `?kind=` and `?status=` filter parameters on list); default UI layout with two natural additions parallel to reference_book — Kind column in the master pane and Status filter combo in the master-pane toolbar; default soft-delete with restore (distinct from cancelled and consumed status outcomes); closed four-value kind enum (`kickoff_prompt`, `claude_code_prompt`, `ad_hoc_prompt`, `other`) with `other` as the documented escape hatch for kinds that emerge in operation; sixteen testable acceptance criteria.
session.artifacts_produced:
Three files committed to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ in the crmbuilder repo during this conversation:

(1) governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md (471 lines, v1.0). Fourth per-entity schema specification in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Conforms to governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Establishes the work_ticket entity type for V2 storage with six content/classification fields (identifier, title, description, notes, kind, status), one file-pointer field (file_path), and seven timestamp columns (three inherited base plus four per-status lifecycle: work_ticket_ready_at, work_ticket_consumed_at, work_ticket_cancelled_at, work_ticket_superseded_at). Five-status workflow-shaped lifecycle (drafted → ready → consumed plus terminals cancelled and superseded) with truly-terminal terminals, the supersession-requires-edge rule inherited from DEC-125, and a new consumed-requires-edge rule establishing the inverse-direction terminal-edge-requirement precedent. Single-use enforcement rule (at most one inbound consumption edge per work_ticket, regardless of status) as the schema-level realization of DEC-117's family-2 definition. Four-value closed kind enum (kickoff_prompt, claude_code_prompt, ad_hoc_prompt, other) with the sentinel as documented escape hatch. Standard 8-endpoint API set with `?kind=` and `?status=` filter parameters. Default UI layout with master-pane Kind column (third column position) and Status filter combo in the toolbar. Default soft-delete with restore. Sixteen acceptance criteria. No new relationship-kind vocabulary required — the entity type itself was already named by conversation.md §3.3.4 and the inbound consumption edge by conversation.md's `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` registration. Pre-existed as an untracked sandbox file at session open; adopted without modification after audit.

(2) close-out-payloads/ses_051.json (new — this payload). One session (SES-051), six decisions (DEC-141..146), zero planning items, seven references (six decided_in plus one is_about SES-051 → PI-022).

(3) prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-051.md (new — this payload's apply prompt). Idempotent apply targeting the CRMBUILDER engagement.

Via this payload's apply prompt, the following records land in the CRMBUILDER engagement database:

(4) SES-051 — this session record.
(5) DEC-141 — WT identifier prefix and format.
(6) DEC-142 — boundary discipline with reference_book (intent-at-creation, no re-categorization, file_path coexistence).
(7) DEC-143 — workflow-shaped lifecycle, five statuses, truly-terminal terminals, consumed-requires-edge rule (new cross-spec precedent), supersession-requires-edge rule.
(8) DEC-144 — single-use enforcement (at most one inbound consumption edge).
(9) DEC-145 — field inventory with four per-status lifecycle timestamps; declines tentative `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` kind; declines typed `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` kind.
(10) DEC-146 — API surface; UI defaults with Kind column and Status filter combo; soft-delete posture; closed four-value kind enum; sixteen acceptance criteria.
(11) Six decided_in references (DEC-141..146 → SES-051) plus one is_about reference (SES-051 → PI-022) mirroring SES-048/049/050's pattern of linking each schema-design conversation to the retroactive-backfill planning item.

No source code modified — this is a schema-design conversation producing one specification only. Vocabulary changes, schema migrations, and access-layer methods are aggregated by the build-planning conversation after all six schemas exist.
session.in_flight_at_end: Next workstream conversation: close_out_payload entity schema design (fifth per-entity schema-design conversation, of six). Opens against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-close-out-payload.md. That conversation produces governance-schema-specs/close_out_payload.md and inherits the six cross-spec precedents now in force: the three from workstream (references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships, per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped lifecycles, terminal-states-are-terminal discipline), the one from conversation (typed sequencing edges introduced when entity-family frequency justifies), the one from reference_book (documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only — workflow-shape per-status timestamps remain the default), and the one new precedent from this conversation (terminal-state consumption requires the inbound consumption edge — the inverse-direction analogue of supersession-requires-edge). The close_out_payload entity is structurally most directly related to this conversation's new precedent: its anticipated `applied` terminal status is the next case of an external-act-of-consumption-defined terminal, and the inbound edge from a deposit_event record naming the apply event is the parallel of work_ticket's inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge. After close_out_payload.md lands, one more schema-design conversation follows (deposit_event). After all six schema specifications exist, the build-planning conversation opens against governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md and produces the integrating Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, per-slice build prompts, and the consolidated vocab.py update aggregating relationship-kind additions from all six specs. Multi-tenancy routing fix slice B and the Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 conversation continue in parallel as independent workstreams per DEC-122.

decisions.title: work_ticket entity identifier prefix and format: WT-NNN, two-letter form, no collision with WS or remaining-two working prefixes
decisions.context: The work_ticket entity is the fourth of six new governance entities designed in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Its identifier prefix is set against the prefix-collision list extended by DEC-123 (WS), DEC-129 (CONV), and DEC-135 (RB). The kickoff's working assumption was WT; alternatives named were WKT, TIX, TKT. The schema-spec methodology guide section 6 admits 2 to 5 letters; DEC-123 affirmed the two-letter form is acceptable; DEC-135 made the same call for RB. The collision-check requires examining (a) all existing prefixes in the V2 prefix set, (b) the two remaining-conversation working prefixes (COP for close_out_payload, DEP for deposit_event) named in the workstream master plan, and (c) the visual-shape distinguishability of any two-letter candidate against neighboring two-letter prefixes.
decisions.decision: **Adopt `WT` as the work_ticket identifier prefix, format `WT-NNN` zero-padded to three digits, server-assigned on POST omission with helper at GET /work-tickets/next-identifier.** The two-letter form is consistent with DEC-123 (WS) and DEC-135 (RB). The collision check confirms: no existing prefix begins with W other than WS (workstream), and the two-letter WT versus two-letter WS differs in the second character with visually-distinct shapes — W-T versus W-S read unambiguously side by side. The two remaining-conversation working prefixes (COP, DEP) do not begin with W. The longer-form alternatives WKT, TIX, TKT add no value beyond length and TIX/TKT carry connotations from ticket-tracking systems (Jira, Trello, GitHub Issues) that the project deliberately does not invoke — 'work ticket' in this project is a single-use seed document, not an issue-tracking artifact.
decisions.rationale: WT is mnemonic ('work ticket' → WT), short enough to scan rapidly in tables and references, and unambiguous against the existing prefix set. The two-letter form is consistent with DEC-123's affirmation that two-letter prefixes are acceptable and matches DEC-135's RB call. The W-T versus W-S second-character difference produces visually distinct shapes (T has a horizontal top stroke; S is curved) and reads cleanly in mixed contexts. The longer-form alternatives offer no semantic improvement and would have made every work_ticket identifier two characters longer for no payoff. TIX and TKT additionally carry external connotations that mischaracterize the entity — the project's work_tickets are single-use seed documents, not Jira-style issues with assignees and triage states.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — WKT.** Three letters. Rejected because it adds one character with no value; the collision check did not surface a need for additional disambiguation. **Option (c) — TIX.** Three letters with ticket-tracking-system connotations. Rejected because the connotations mischaracterize the entity (work_tickets are single-use seed documents, not Jira-style issues). **Option (d) — TKT.** Three letters, similar to TIX. Rejected for the same connotation reason and additionally because TKT reads as a phonetic abbreviation rather than a meaningful initialism. **Option (e) — Defer the prefix choice to build-planning.** Rejected because the prefix is needed in the spec body (acceptance criteria reference WT-NNN format) and the spec's per-spec completeness gate requires the prefix locked in §3.1.
decisions.consequences: Spec guide section 6 collision list extended with WT. The kickoff's working-assumption prefix is confirmed. The remaining two governance entity conversations (close_out_payload, deposit_event) have first refusal on their own prefix choices; the working prefixes COP and DEP do not collide with WT but each conversation makes its own call. The work_ticket table's identifier column has CHECK constraint `^WT-\d{3}$`. The GET /work-tickets/next-identifier helper returns the next available WT-NNN value.

decisions.title: work_ticket boundary discipline with reference_book: intent-at-creation classification, no in-place re-categorization, file_path may coexist across both tables
decisions.context: The kickoff named the boundary with reference_book as the principal design question for the work_ticket entity, given that both entity types are workflow files with similar surface fields (title, description, file_path, status). The distinction sits in lifecycle and reference pattern: work_ticket is single-use (produced for one conversation, consumed by it, not subsequently referenced as a source of truth); reference_book is long-lived and multi-consumer (cited by many conversations as authoritative reference). The kickoff named three candidate boundary policies: (a) bright line by intent — authorial intent at creation time settles the classification; (b) bright line by lifecycle — work_ticket if no version after consumption, reference_book if it has versions; (c) re-categorization — allow a work_ticket to be re-categorized to a reference_book after the fact if it turns out to be referenced repeatedly. The kickoff also named a boundary case: the methodology workstream master plan was produced by SES-011, whose kickoff (`ui-v0.4-planning-prompt.md`) was retroactively cited by successor planning documents — is that retroactive forward-referencing enough to reclassify the kickoff as a reference_book?
decisions.decision: **Adopt bright line by intent at creation, with no in-place re-categorization, and explicit support for parallel coexistence of work_ticket and reference_book records pointing at the same `file_path`.** A file is a work_ticket if it was authored as a single-use seed for one specific conversation; it is a reference_book if it was authored as a long-lived multi-consumer reference. The classification is settled at creation and does not change. If a file authored as a work_ticket turns out to be referenced repeatedly enough to warrant authoritative-reference treatment, a separate reference_book record is authored against the same `file_path`; the two records coexist as independent governance views with different identifiers, different lifecycles, and different fields. The schema does NOT enforce mutual exclusivity at the `work_ticket_file_path` / `reference_book_file_path` level — each column is unique within its own table, not unique across the union. The methodology workstream master-plan boundary case raised in the kickoff (`ui-v0.4-planning-prompt.md`) resolves as a work_ticket; the retroactive forward-referencing by successor planning documents is archival mention, not authoritative reading.
decisions.rationale: Intent-at-creation is the simplest rule that maps cleanly to the project's actual authoring pattern. Every kickoff and every CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md file is authored with single-use intent for one specific conversation or execution; that intent is captured at creation and does not change even if the file is later mentioned by successor documents. Re-categorization (option c) would require the schema to admit moving records between tables, which forces a version-history-of-the-classification-itself concern that adds complexity without operational payoff — the catalog of work_tickets and the catalog of reference_books should each be stable. Lifecycle-based classification (option b) would conflate the versioning model (reference_book has child versions; work_ticket does not) with the family definition (single-use vs multi-consumer), which are independently meaningful and should not be collapsed. Parallel coexistence (the same file_path string in both tables) is the natural resolution when a file genuinely spans both governance views: the kickoff's archival role as 'the file that opened SES-011' lives on a work_ticket record; if the file's role evolves to 'the document successor planning conversations cite authoritatively,' a parallel reference_book record is authored and both records coexist. Uniqueness scoped to each table preserves the schema's simplicity.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — bright line by lifecycle (work_ticket if no version after consumption, reference_book if it has versions).** Rejected because it conflates the versioning model with the family definition; a work_ticket's lifecycle doesn't end at consumption (the record persists with status `consumed`), and not all reference_books accumulate versions in practice. **Option (c) — re-categorization (move records between tables if the use pattern changes).** Rejected because moving records between tables forces a classification-history concern (which table did this live in last week? what queries return it?) that adds operational complexity without payoff; parallel coexistence achieves the same effect more cleanly. **Option (d) — single-table polymorphism with a discriminator column.** Rejected as premature schema unification; the two entities have meaningfully different field inventories (work_ticket has consumed-requires-edge and single-use rules; reference_book has version-pointer columns and a child versions table) and lifecycle semantics; collapsing them would obscure both. **Option (e) — mutual exclusivity at the file_path level (a file_path may exist in at most one of the two tables).** Rejected because parallel coexistence is the natural resolution when a file genuinely spans both governance views; forcing mutual exclusivity would make the kickoff's natural archival-plus-authoritative pattern unrepresentable.
decisions.consequences: Schema enforces per-table uniqueness on file_path (work_ticket_file_path unique within work_tickets; reference_book_file_path unique within reference_books) but does not enforce cross-table uniqueness. PI-022's backfill pass classifies each historical workflow file by intent at creation; ambiguous cases default to work_ticket (the more conservative classification — a work_ticket can be supplemented by a parallel reference_book record later, but a reference_book record carries versioning machinery that may not fit single-use seeds). The methodology workstream master plan example (ui-v0.4-planning-prompt.md) is backfilled as a work_ticket; if successor citation patterns warrant a parallel reference_book record, it is authored as a separate operation. Section 2 of the spec carries the boundary-discipline narrative in full; section 3.8.2 captures the backfill-policy details for PI-022 to resolve. The build-planning conversation may add a cross-table sanity-check query (e.g., 'show all file_paths that exist in both tables') as a maintenance tool, but no schema-level constraint is needed.

decisions.title: work_ticket workflow-shaped lifecycle with five statuses, truly terminal terminals, consumed-requires-edge rule (new cross-spec precedent), and supersession-requires-edge rule
decisions.context: The work_ticket entity's lifecycle expresses the single-use seed document's progression from authoring through consumption (or abandonment). DEC-137 from SES-050 settled the documentary-vs-workflow distinction: documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only (reference_book's posture); workflow-shaped lifecycles carry per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-126. Work_ticket is workflow-shaped — consultants ask 'when did this kickoff become ready?' and 'when did the conversation consume it?'; the transitions are real-time-meaningful events. The kickoff named a working set of five status values (drafted, ready, consumed, superseded, cancelled) and asked whether all five are needed and what transitions are valid. Additionally, the kickoff's family-definitional question — what distinguishes work_ticket from reference_book at the schema level — points at the `consumed` status as the family-defining terminal: the act of consumption is what binds a work_ticket to its one conversation, and the schema needs to enforce that binding.
decisions.decision: **(a) Adopt a five-status workflow-shaped lifecycle: `drafted` → `ready` → `consumed`, plus two abandonment terminals reachable from drafted or ready: `cancelled` and `superseded`.** Default starter status is `drafted`. (b) **Terminal-states-are-terminal discipline inherited from DEC-125 verbatim.** All three terminals (consumed, cancelled, superseded) reject every outgoing transition, including transitions between terminal states. A consumed work_ticket cannot become superseded even if the kickoff is later rewritten — the rewrite is a new work_ticket. (c) **Per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-126.** Four per-status timestamp columns (work_ticket_ready_at, work_ticket_consumed_at, work_ticket_cancelled_at, work_ticket_superseded_at), server-set on transition, mutually exclusive among the three terminal-state columns (exactly one is populated on any terminal record). (d) **Supersession-requires-edge rule inherited verbatim from DEC-125.** Setting status to `superseded` requires an outgoing `supersedes` edge to another work_ticket record; access layer enforces with HTTP 422 `{"error": "supersession_requires_successor_edge"}`. (e) **NEW — Consumed-requires-edge rule.** Setting status to `consumed` requires an inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge from a conversation record; access layer enforces with HTTP 422 `{"error": "consumed_work_ticket_requires_consumption_edge"}`. This is the inverse-direction analogue of the supersession-requires-edge rule: supersession-requires-edge requires an outgoing successor edge; consumed-requires-edge requires an inbound consumer edge. **Establishes new cross-spec precedent**: for any governance entity with a terminal state defined by an external act of consumption or application, the transition to that terminal requires the edge naming the consumer or applier to be present, in whichever direction (inbound or outbound) the relationship is canonically modelled. Close_out_payload's anticipated `applied` terminal is the next case (inbound edge from deposit_event required); deposit_event's append-only-with-one-timestamp shape sidesteps the question (the record's creation IS the apply event).
decisions.rationale: Five statuses capture the operational vocabulary cleanly: drafted (authoring in progress, file may be incomplete), ready (file committed, conversation can be opened), consumed (conversation opened against this work_ticket — terminal), cancelled (abandoned without consumption — terminal), superseded (abandoned with a successor work_ticket — terminal). Collapsing drafted and ready would lose the visibility into 'what kickoffs are ready to open' versus 'what kickoffs are still being authored' — a real consultant query. Collapsing cancelled and superseded would lose the distinction between 'abandoned outright' and 'abandoned with a successor carrying the work forward' — a meaningful authorial signal. Truly-terminal terminals match the precedent from DEC-125 and DEC-131; the project's discipline that a terminal entity's role-resumption is modelled as a new record (not an edit) is preserved. Per-status timestamps fit the workflow-shaped lifecycle per DEC-126; the consultant queries the timestamps answer ('when did this become ready?' 'when was it consumed?') are real-time-meaningful. The consumed-requires-edge rule is the schema-level realization of the family-definitional binding between a work_ticket and its one consuming conversation: a work_ticket cannot be consumed without the consumer being known. The cross-spec precedent generalizes naturally: any terminal defined by an external act requires the edge naming the actor; the direction of the edge follows from the verb-orientation of the relationship.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Four-status lifecycle collapsing drafted and ready.** Rejected because the 'ready to open' versus 'still authoring' distinction is operationally meaningful and asked about by consultants. **Four-status lifecycle collapsing cancelled and superseded.** Rejected because the 'abandoned outright' versus 'abandoned with successor' distinction is a meaningful authorial signal and informs the supersedes edge. **Three-status lifecycle (drafted, consumed, abandoned).** Rejected as too coarse — loses ready-to-open visibility and supersession distinction. **Workflow lifecycle without terminal-edge requirements (rely on the references-create dialog's filtering to prevent invalid states).** Rejected because the references-create dialog cannot enforce status-level invariants from outside the access layer; the consumed-requires-edge and supersession-requires-edge rules must live at the access layer to be reliable. **Reactivation-from-terminal admitted (e.g., a consumed work_ticket can be reset to ready if the conversation's first opening was a false start).** Rejected because the project's discipline that role-resumption is modelled as a new record is well-established and reactivation would complicate the lifecycle without operational payoff; a false-start consumption is modelled as soft-deleting the work_ticket administratively and creating a fresh record.
decisions.consequences: Five-value CHECK constraint on work_ticket_status. Four per-status timestamp columns added to the schema. Access-layer enforcement of (a) valid-transition map per §3.4.1, (b) truly-terminal posture (no transitions out of terminal states), (c) consumed-requires-edge rule with HTTP 422 on POST/PATCH violation and DELETE-edge-while-consumed violation, (d) supersession-requires-edge rule with HTTP 422 on POST/PATCH violation and DELETE-edge-while-superseded violation. The new consumed-requires-edge precedent is captured for the remaining two schema-design conversations to inherit; close_out_payload's `applied` terminal will follow the same pattern with the inbound edge from deposit_event. Spec §3.4 carries the full lifecycle specification including transition map, transition semantics, both edge-requirement rules, the single-use enforcement rule (DEC-144 separately), kind-enum semantics, and soft-delete semantics. Acceptance criteria 5-7 and 9-10 (spec §3.7) translate the lifecycle and edge rules into testable statements.

decisions.title: work_ticket single-use enforcement rule: at most one inbound conversation_opens_against_work_ticket edge per work_ticket, regardless of status
decisions.context: DEC-117 defines work_ticket as family 2 of the three workflow-file families: single-use seed documents with at most one consumer. The family-definitional property is the single-use semantics — a work_ticket is produced for one specific conversation, consumed by that conversation, and not subsequently referenced as a source of truth. The schema must express this single-use property as a hard constraint at the access layer; otherwise the family-definitional property is documented but not enforced, and historical or accidental multi-consumer attachments would silently corrupt the family classification. The consumed-requires-edge rule (DEC-143) requires the inbound edge to exist at consumption, but does not bound the count of inbound edges. The single-use property requires an upper bound (≤ 1) on inbound edges of the consumption kind.
decisions.decision: **Enforce at the access layer: a work_ticket record may have at most one inbound edge of kind `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket`, regardless of the work_ticket's current status.** POST attempting to add a second inbound consumption edge to a work_ticket that already has one returns HTTP 422 `{"error": "work_ticket_single_use_violation"}`. The rule applies at all statuses: a drafted or ready work_ticket may have zero inbound edges; a consumed work_ticket has exactly one; a cancelled or superseded work_ticket may have zero (the typical case — abandoned before consumption) but may also have one if a partial consumption occurred before abandonment. The rule never admits two or more.
decisions.rationale: The single-use semantics defined by DEC-117 are family-definitional and must be enforced at the schema level to be reliable. Documenting the property without enforcing it would allow accidental or historical multi-consumer attachments — for example, a backfill script attempting to record two conversations consuming the same kickoff — to silently bypass the family classification. The 422 error surfaces the violation immediately, forcing the consumer to choose between (a) using the existing single-use work_ticket for the right conversation and creating a new work_ticket for the second consumer, or (b) reclassifying the file as a reference_book if it genuinely had multiple authoritative consumers. The status-independence of the rule prevents corner cases where a cancelled work_ticket might be reused or where a consumed work_ticket might accidentally accumulate a second consumption edge. The rule is also what schema-level-distinguishes work_ticket from reference_book: reference_book admits arbitrarily many inbound edges (each `workstream_planned_in_reference_book` from a workstream that cites it as master plan, each generic citation from a conversation, etc.); work_ticket admits exactly one of the typed consumption kind.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Status-conditional rule (single-use enforced only on consumed status).** Rejected because it permits a drafted or ready work_ticket to accumulate two or more inbound consumption edges before consumption, which is operationally ill-defined (which conversation 'really' consumed it?) and forces ad-hoc resolution at status-transition time. **Documenting the property without access-layer enforcement.** Rejected because family-definitional invariants must be reliable; documentation alone permits backfill or accidental writes to bypass the family classification silently. **Enforcing the rule via a database-level UNIQUE constraint on (target_id, relationship='conversation_opens_against_work_ticket').** Rejected because the references table is generic (no per-kind unique constraints); the access layer is the right place for kind-specific invariants. **Allowing multiple inbound consumption edges but flagging in a maintenance view.** Rejected for the same reason as 'documenting only' — the invariant must be enforced, not surfaced after the fact.
decisions.consequences: Access-layer write path for the `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` relationship kind checks the target work_ticket's existing inbound edge count before admitting the write. The check is a single COUNT query against the references table filtered by target_type='work_ticket', target_id=<the_work_ticket>, relationship='conversation_opens_against_work_ticket'; the write is admitted only if the count is zero. Acceptance criterion 8 (spec §3.7) translates the rule into a testable statement. The rule applies regardless of the work_ticket's status (acceptance criterion explicitly tests this). PI-022's backfill pass must respect the rule; ambiguous historical cases (a file used by two conversations before this entity type existed) are resolved per spec §3.8.2 — either by authoring one work_ticket with the first consumption typed and the second as a generic `references` edge, or by authoring two work_ticket records with different identifiers pointing at the same file_path. The schema admits the latter per DEC-142's parallel-coexistence rule (uniqueness on file_path is per-table, not cross-table).

decisions.title: work_ticket field inventory with four per-status lifecycle timestamps; declines tentative work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation kind; declines typed work_ticket_reads_reference_book kind
decisions.context: Three field-inventory and vocabulary items decided at the close of the work_ticket schema-design conversation: (a) the complete field inventory beyond identifier, status, and base timestamps; (b) whether to introduce the tentative inbound relationship-kind `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` named by conversation.md §3.3.2 as a candidate; (c) whether to introduce a typed `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` kind for the kickoff-prompt read-list citation pattern (every kickoff prompt names a 'Read this first' list of reference books). Each item is structurally a vocabulary or schema-shape decision that the spec must lock before the per-spec completeness gate is met.
decisions.decision: **(a) Field inventory (eleven columns total):** identity fields (work_ticket_identifier, work_ticket_title); content fields (work_ticket_description, work_ticket_notes); classification fields (work_ticket_kind, work_ticket_status); no foreign-key columns (every relationship lives in `refs` per DEC-124's references-edge precedent); file pointer (work_ticket_file_path); three inherited base timestamps (work_ticket_created_at, work_ticket_updated_at, work_ticket_deleted_at); four per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-126 for workflow-shaped lifecycle (work_ticket_ready_at, work_ticket_consumed_at, work_ticket_cancelled_at, work_ticket_superseded_at). Deliberately excluded under minimum-viable scope: work_ticket_author and work_ticket_owner (Doug sole today, one migration away), work_ticket_target_deliverable (folded into description), work_ticket_engagement_scope (per-engagement isolation implicit per DEC-115/116), content-blob storage (file lives in git), sub-kind enum (the work_ticket_kind enum is closed at four values for this release). **(b) Decline tentative kind `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation`.** Conversation.md §3.3.2 named it as a candidate inbound-from-work_ticket-perspective name for the same relationship that conversation.md §3.3.1 already declared as outbound-from-conversation-perspective via `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket`. The existing outbound kind is the sole canonical edge for the relationship, viewed from work_ticket's side as inbound. Introducing a parallel inbound name would create two relationship-kind values for the same edge and break the one-edge-per-relationship pattern enforced by the source-first naming convention (DEC-048). **(c) Decline typed kind `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` for read-list citations.** Every kickoff prompt names a 'Read this first' list of reference books; the citation pattern is structural and recurs across all kickoff prompts. However, applying DEC-133's frequency-justified test: the question 'what work_tickets read reference book RB-042' is operational metadata about authoring, not a queried-by structure (consultants don't run that query at scale). The generic `references` kind covers the citation adequately. If queries by read-list become routine in operation, a future release adds the typed kind with the matching vocab and access-layer support (one-line vocab.py + backfill pass converting existing generic edges).
decisions.rationale: **(a) Field inventory** follows minimum-viable per the methodology guide; the four per-status lifecycle timestamps fit DEC-126's workflow-shaped lifecycle precedent (work_ticket is workflow-shaped per DEC-143). Deferred fields are listed in spec §3.8.3 with growth-condition language so future signal can justify additions. The target-deliverable description is folded into work_ticket_description because most kickoffs name the target deliverable in the first paragraph of their description; a separate column would duplicate the narrative. **(b) Declining the tentative kind** preserves the one-edge-per-relationship pattern. The source-first naming convention (DEC-048) settles direction: the conversation entity is the actor that 'opens against' the work_ticket; the kind name expresses the conversation's verb, with work_ticket on the receiving side. Introducing a parallel inbound name would force every consumer of the references table to handle two equivalent forms of the same edge. The decline mirrors DEC-132's tentative-kind-name resolution from SES-049 (where conversation.md declined the tentative `conversation_succeeds_conversation` inverse name). **(c) Declining the typed read-list kind** is the application of DEC-133's frequency-justified test: typed sequencing or relationship kinds are introduced when the entity-family frequency of the query justifies the schema-level investment. Read-list citations are authored once at kickoff drafting time and rarely queried-by; the generic kind suffices. The retrofit cost is low (one line of vocab + a backfill pass converting existing generic edges to the typed kind), so deferring is non-prohibitive if the query pattern emerges.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Field inventory with separate work_ticket_target_deliverable column.** Rejected because the target deliverable is almost always named in the first paragraph of work_ticket_description; a separate column duplicates the narrative. **Field inventory with work_ticket_author column.** Rejected for minimum-viable scope; Doug is sole author today. **Adopt the tentative `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` kind in addition to the conversation-side `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` kind.** Rejected because it breaks the one-edge-per-relationship pattern and would force consumers to handle two equivalent forms. **Adopt the typed `work_ticket_reads_reference_book` kind in this release.** Rejected because the query frequency does not justify schema-level investment per DEC-133's precedent; the generic `references` kind is adequate. **Adopt a sub-kind taxonomy below the four named work_ticket_kind values (e.g., kickoff_prompt with sub-kinds session_prompt, update_prompt; claude_code_prompt with sub-kinds apply_prompt, build_prompt).** Rejected as premature taxonomy specialization; the four values cover the project's observed work-ticket families and a sub-kind axis can be added in a future release if querying by it becomes routine.
decisions.consequences: Work_tickets table has eleven columns. CHECK constraints on identifier format (`^WT-\d{3}$`), kind enum (four values), status enum (five values), file_path validation (non-empty trimmed, no leading slash, no `..` segments, no scheme prefix). Per-status timestamp columns are nullable and server-set on transition; the consumed/cancelled/superseded triple is mutually exclusive on any terminal record. No new relationship-kind vocabulary added by this spec; the entity type `work_ticket` is already listed in conversation.md's additions table and the existing generic kinds plus the conversation-side `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` cover every relationship work_ticket needs. PI-022's backfill pass populates the eleven content/classification/file-pointer/timestamp fields for historical work_ticket records; the backfill policy on historical multi-consumer cases and unknown timestamps is PI-022's call per spec §3.8.2.

decisions.title: work_ticket API surface, default UI with Kind column and Status filter combo, soft-delete posture, closed four-value kind enum, sixteen acceptance criteria
decisions.context: Routine items batched together at the close of the work_ticket schema-design conversation: the API surface beyond the standard endpoint set, the UI layout including the master-pane Kind column and the toolbar Status filter combo, the soft-delete posture relative to the cancelled and consumed status outcomes, the kind enum membership and the `other` sentinel semantics, and the count and shape of testable acceptance criteria. None of these is architecturally novel relative to the schema-spec methodology guide's defaults or the precedents established by DEC-128/134/140, but each warrants explicit decision recording because they shape API surface and UI behavior.
decisions.decision: **(a) API surface** is the standard 8-endpoint set per spec guide §3.5 (GET list with `?include_deleted=true` plus `?kind=` plus `?status=` filters; GET single; POST create; PUT replace; PATCH update; DELETE soft-delete; POST restore; GET next-identifier). No version-management sub-endpoints (work_ticket is single-use, not versioned). All endpoints return the V2 `{data, meta, errors}` envelope. **(b) Identifier auto-assignment**: default server-side on POST omission; helper at GET /work-tickets/next-identifier. **(c) Master pane** has five columns: Identifier, Title, Kind, Status, Updated. Default sort by Identifier ascending. The Kind column is the third column (between Title and Status) — operationally meaningful classification, parallel to reference_book's pattern. Kind selector and Status selector combo boxes in the panel toolbar drive `?kind=` and `?status=` filters; both default to 'all'. The Status filter combo is the primary operational filter per the kickoff's note that browse-by-status is the work_ticket's natural pattern. **(d) Detail pane** has nine sections in order: identifier (read-only), title, description, notes (collapsible internal section, collapsed by default), kind combo, file_path, status combo, lifecycle timestamps section (read-only labels rendered conditionally for non-null timestamps), ReferencesSection. **(e) CRUD dialogs** match the user-interface version 0.3 governance-entity patterns: EntityCrudDialog subclass for create/edit, EntityCrudDeleteDialog with edge-text confirmation for delete. Edit dialog detects missing consumption edge when transitioning to `consumed` and surfaces an inline hint; same for missing supersedes edge when transitioning to `superseded`. **(f) Soft-delete posture**: default — soft-delete with restore — explicitly distinct from `cancelled` status (a lifecycle outcome — work_ticket was abandoned without being consumed) and `consumed` status (a successful-use outcome — the conversation opened against it). **(g) Kind enum**: closed four-value enum (`kickoff_prompt`, `claude_code_prompt`, `ad_hoc_prompt`, `other`). The `other` sentinel is the documented escape hatch for kinds that emerge in operation. The CBM `SESSION-PROMPT-*` and `UPDATE-PROMPT-*` filename conventions are captured as `kickoff_prompt` for this release with the distinction recorded in title or description; a future release adds first-class enum values if those families warrant. **(h) Acceptance criteria**: sixteen testable statements captured in spec §3.7 (gate requires ten; this spec matches workstream's sixteen, conversation's eighteen, reference_book's sixteen), covering schema migration, identifier and kind and status enum validation, terminal-states-truly-terminal, consumed-requires-edge rule, single-use enforcement rule, supersession-requires-edge rule, lifecycle timestamp server-set behavior, file_path repo-relative validation, access-layer method signatures, REST endpoint behavior with kind and status filter parameters, identifier auto-assignment, soft-delete and restore round-trip, and one end-to-end backfill exemplar against this conversation's own work_ticket.
decisions.rationale: Standard endpoint set inherited from the methodology guide; no deviations warranted because the entity's operations follow the standard CRUD-plus-restore pattern. No version-management sub-endpoints because work_ticket is single-use (the file is consumed once and not subsequently versioned). The Kind column is a natural addition parallel to reference_book's; consultants ask 'what apply-close-out prompts are queued' or 'what kickoffs are ready,' and kind plus status are the two operational axes. The Status filter combo is the primary operational filter — a consultant browsing the work_ticket catalog typically wants to see what is `ready` to be opened (the kickoff's note explicitly named browse-by-status as the natural pattern); the toolbar combo serves this without committing to a status-first default sort that would diverge from reference_book's identifier-first cross-spec consistency. Conditional rendering of lifecycle timestamps in the detail pane (only non-null timestamps shown as read-only labels) keeps the pane clean across the lifecycle. Soft-delete posture matches workstream/conversation/reference_book precedents and is explicitly distinct from the two lifecycle outcomes (cancelled, consumed). The closed four-value kind enum covers every work_ticket observed in the project's history; the `other` sentinel preserves additive extensibility. Sixteen acceptance criteria match reference_book's and workstream's counts and fully satisfy the gate; each is concrete and testable, ready for translation into test cases at build-planning time.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **API surface with version-management sub-endpoints.** Rejected because work_ticket is single-use, not versioned; the sub-endpoints would have no semantics. **Master pane without Kind column.** Rejected because kind is the most operationally meaningful classification for browsing the work_ticket catalog. **Master pane with status-first default sort (per the kickoff's hint).** Rejected in favour of the partial adoption — status is the fourth column (visible without scrolling) and the Status filter combo serves the browse-by-status pattern, but the default sort remains identifier-ascending for cross-spec consistency with workstream/conversation/reference_book. **Detail pane without conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering (always show all four columns even when null).** Rejected because nullable timestamps cluttered the pane unnecessarily — the conditional rendering matches the underlying data and stays clean. **Open-string kind field instead of closed enum.** Rejected per DEC-138's precedent that closed enums with `other` sentinels are the structural pattern for kind classifications. **Six-value kind enum naming session_prompt and update_prompt as first-class values.** Rejected as premature speciation; the CBM filename conventions are captured as kickoff_prompt with title/description distinction for this release. **Acceptance criteria count of ten (gate minimum).** Rejected because the consumed-requires-edge rule, single-use enforcement rule, and end-to-end backfill exemplar deserve dedicated criteria; sixteen matches the project's working baseline.
decisions.consequences: Eight REST endpoints to test; sixteen test cases per the acceptance criteria. The list endpoint's `?kind=` and `?status=` filter parameters combine additively (both filters narrow the result set). Master-pane Kind column requires the friendly-label mapping for the four enum values (e.g., 'Kickoff prompt' for `kickoff_prompt`). The detail-pane's conditional lifecycle-timestamp rendering follows the underlying nullable columns; the references-section displays the inbound `conversation_opens_against_work_ticket` edge prominently as the family-defining relationship per spec §3.6.3. Edit dialog hint logic for missing consumption/supersedes edges adds a small UX layer atop the access-layer 422 enforcement. Soft-delete is administrative and orthogonal to the lifecycle statuses; the operational vocabulary distinguishes 'archive an erroneous record' (soft-delete) from 'this work_ticket was abandoned' (cancelled status) from 'this work_ticket was consumed' (consumed status). The spec's per-spec completeness gate (spec guide §7.1) passes: all nine subsections of §3 substantively filled, sixteen acceptance criteria, explicit category tags on open questions, cross-references include workstream plan, spec guide, workstream.md and conversation.md and reference_book.md as predecessor specifications, foundation decisions DEC-117..122, and the eighteen prior-conversation decisions DEC-123..140 from SES-048/049/050 as inherited precedents.
